executive_summary: Data blackout Friday: the production database, Search Console and GA4 all failed, so there are zero order, revenue or customer numbers today. Google Ads was the only working source — $92.95 spent on 65 clicks and 1,010 impressions, below the $103.32 daily 7-day average.
analyst_brief:
**The one thing today is a broken pipeline, not a growth call.** Almost every data source failed this morning. The production database didn't load because a required library (pymysql) isn't installed on the machine, Search Console failed on a missing Google module, and GA4/Stripe/Intercom aren't wired in. That means we have no orders, no revenue, no new-customer count, no ratings, no cancellations — nothing to base a recommendation on. Please get pymysql installed and the Search Console module fixed before tomorrow's 6 AM run, otherwise we lose another day blind. Without the database I genuinely can't tell you whether yesterday was a good day or a bad one.

Here's what the one working source — Google Ads — actually showed, kept separate because it can't be tied back to real orders:

- We spent **$92.95** on Friday, a bit under the **$103.32** average daily spend over the last 7 days. That bought 65 clicks and 1,010 impressions.
- Google reported **7 "conversions,"** but remember these count sign-ups, not paid orders. Without the database we can't turn that into a real cost per new customer, so treat the 7 as a soft signal only.
- The GTA-wide click campaign ate roughly half the budget ($45.69) for 36 clicks and 2 sign-ups. Hamilton was efficient — $11.68 for 1 sign-up, and the search "laundry service hamilton" both got clicked and converted.
- One thing worth watching, **as an observation only**: a big chunk of spend went to "laundromat near me" and similar searches — at least four separate clicks totalling around $12 — with zero sign-ups. These are people hunting for a physical laundromat to walk into, not our pickup-and-delivery service, so the click rarely fits what we offer. I am **not** recommending any keyword changes here — that needs your manual review, and our past negative-keyword changes have blocked good searches. Just flagging it for a human to look at when there's time.
- Richmond Hill showed 28 impressions but got 0 clicks and spent nothing — effectively dormant.

That's the whole picture today. The honest summary: one day of partial ad data, no business numbers, and an infrastructure fix that needs to happen before we can advise on anything. Once the database is back, tomorrow's brief can actually answer "what's the highest-leverage move."
validation_notes: No DB cross-check possible: the production database failed to load (pymysql not installed), so there are no order or revenue figures to validate against Stripe. Search Console, GA4, Stripe and Intercom were also unavailable. Only Google Ads reported.
data_footnotes: Production DB: UNAVAILABLE — query-db.py failed, pymysql not installed. No orders, revenue, customers, ratings, cancellations, or failed payments available.
Google Ads: account 972-828-9560, available. Friday 2026-06-19 spend $92.95 CAD, 7d total $723.25, 7d avg daily $103.32. 'Conversions' count registrations, not purchases.
Search Console: UNAVAILABLE — 'No module named google'. Indexing health for 42 monitored pages could not be assessed.
Stripe: UNAVAILABLE (no API key) — DB-vs-Stripe cross-check not possible.
ClickUp / Intercom: UNAVAILABLE (Phase 2 / no API key).
computed{} block was empty this run — no 7-day averages, percentage changes, or CPA were pre-computed, so all scorecard rows are marked n/a rather than fabricated.
Revenue methodology (when available): all monetary values net of 13% HST, tips excluded.

days.executive_summary: Solid Thursday. Delivered revenue hit $837.75 ex. HST (+32.1% vs 7d avg) on 13 orders with big baskets ($64.44 delivered AOV). Placed orders flat at 12. The sore spot: cost per new customer was $36.93 — well above the $25 ceiling — and the 7-day rolling figure ($47.46) is worse.
days.analyst_brief:
Yesterday was a good revenue day. We delivered 13 orders worth $837.75 ex. HST, about a third above the recent norm, and the orders were unusually large ($64.44 delivered average). Placed orders sat right on the 7-day line at 12. Nothing broke, ratings are still excellent (4.69, one low rating), cancellations are near zero. So the day itself is fine.

The thing worth your attention is where the ad money went. We spent $110.78 and got 3 new customers, which works out to $36.93 each — half again over the $25 ceiling. And the 7-day rolling figure is worse at $47.46, so this isn't a one-day blip; we've been paying too much to acquire customers for a week.

The leak is concentrated in one place. The **"City Target (GTA) Click" campaign spent $62.70 — more than half the entire day's ad budget — and produced zero trackable signups.** Meanwhile small campaigns did the work: Vaughan ($6.49 → 3 registrations) and Calgary ($9.59 → 1) carried the day on pennies. The GTA Click campaign is the single biggest drag on your acquisition cost. I'd take a hard look at it. Note: the registration counts come from Google's tracking, which counts signups, not real orders, so treat them as a rough guide — but a campaign spending the most and showing nothing is a clear pattern, not noise.

On the search-term side, a lot of GTA Click money is flowing to "laundromat near me" variants that aren't converting. I'm flagging this as an observation only — do **not** add negative keywords off this; that needs your manual review (past negatives blocked good searches). Just worth knowing what the budget is buying.

One quieter worry: how many new customers come back. The April groups reordered at 28–41% within three weeks, but the May groups have dropped to single digits (6.7%, 8.3% for the last two weeks shown). The most recent weeks aren't fully mature yet — the 21-day window hasn't closed — so don't over-read it, but the direction is down and it's worth watching. Paying $37–47 to acquire someone only pays off if they come back, so the acquisition-cost problem and the reorder problem compound each other.

On search rankings: Toronto (position 20.9) and Ottawa (11.8) get lots of impressions but barely any clicks because they sit too deep to be seen. The city pages share one template for titles, so this isn't a one-page fix — the body copy is the only per-city lever. Lower priority than the ad spend question.

The one thing today: figure out what the GTA Click campaign is actually doing for $60+/day, because it's the main reason new customers cost more than they should.
